title: Merge Intervals
description: Given a collection of intervals merge all overlapping intervals

examples.explanation: Initial: [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]], n=4. Pass 1: Compare [1,3] with [2,6]: overlap (3>=2 and 1<=6), merge to [1,6], remove [2,6], list becomes [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]], n=3, merged=true. Restart. Pass 2: Compare [1,6] with [8,10]: no overlap (6<8). Compare [1,6] with [15,18]: no overlap (6<15). Compare [8,10] with [15,18]: no overlap (10<15). No merges, merged=false. Done. Output: [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]].

Initial: [[1,4],[4,5]], n=2. Pass 1: Compare [1,4] with [4,5]: overlap (4>=4 and 1<=5), merge to [1,5], remove [4,5], list becomes [[1,5]], n=1, merged=true. Restart. Pass 2: Only one interval, no comparisons, merged=false. Done. Output: [[1,5]].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare every interval with every other interval to find overlaps, merging them iteratively. Use nested loops to repeatedly scan and update the list until no more merges are possible.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if done in-place, otherwise O(n) for output

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Merge Intervals
ladders.desc: Complete solution using nested loops to compare and merge all overlapping intervals
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the merge intervals problem by repeatedly scanning the list and merging any overlapping intervals until no more merges are possible. The intuition is straightforward: if we compare every interval with every other interval, we'll eventually find all overlaps. We maintain a boolean flag to track if any merge occurred during a pass through the list. If a merge happens, we restart the scanning process because the newly merged interval might overlap with others we've already checked. The algorithm uses nested loops: outer loop continues while merges occur, inner loop compares each interval with all subsequent intervals. When two intervals overlap (interval i's end >= interval j's start AND interval i's start <= interval j's end), we merge them by taking the minimum start and maximum end, remove the second interval, and adjust indices. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case where each merge reduces list size by 1, requiring n passes. Space complexity is O(1) if done in-place by modifying the input list, or O(n) if creating new output. This connects to L1's overlap detection as the core subroutine. Common mistakes include: not restarting after merges (missing new overlaps), incorrect overlap condition (must check both directions), and off-by-one errors when removing elements. Real code pattern: while merged flag true, reset flag false, for i from 0 to n-2, for j from i+1 to n-1, if overlap, merge, remove j, decrement n, set flag true, break inner loop.

examples.trace: Initial: [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]], n=4. Pass 1: Compare [1,3] with [2,6]: overlap (3>=2 and 1<=6), merge to [1,6], remove [2,6], list becomes [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]], n=3, merged=true. Restart. Pass 2: Compare [1,6] with [8,10]: no overlap (6<8). Compare [1,6] with [15,18]: no overlap (6<15). Compare [8,10] with [15,18]: no overlap (10<15). No merges, merged=false. Done. Output: [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]].

Initial: [[1,4],[4,5]], n=2. Pass 1: Compare [1,4] with [4,5]: overlap (4>=4 and 1<=5), merge to [1,5], remove [4,5], list becomes [[1,5]], n=1, merged=true. Restart. Pass 2: Only one interval, no comparisons, merged=false. Done. Output: [[1,5]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Interval Overlap Detection and Merging
ladders.desc: Detect if two intervals overlap and merge them into one
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition: two intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap if they share any common points, which occurs when the end of one is greater than or equal to the start of the other, AND the start of one is less than or equal to the end of the other. Mathematically: overlap if (b >= c AND a <= d). This covers all cases: partial overlaps, complete containment, and touching intervals (where b == c or a == d). Once overlap is detected, merging creates a new interval with min(a,c) as start and max(b,d) as end. The algorithm: function overlap_and_merge(interval1, interval2): if interval1.end >= interval2.start AND interval1.start <= interval2.end: return [min(interval1.start, interval2.start), max(interval1.end, interval2.end)] else: return null. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called in nested loops. Common mistakes: using only one condition (e.g., just checking if starts overlap), forgetting to include equality for touching intervals, and incorrect merge (e.g., averaging instead of min/max). Real code pattern: def merge_two(i1, i2): if i1[1] >= i2[0] and i1[0] <= i2[1]: return [min(i1[0], i2[0]), max(i1[1], i2[1])]; return None.

examples.trace: Check overlap: 3 >= 2 (true) and 1 <= 6 (true) → overlap. Merge: min(1,2)=1, max(3,6)=6 → [1,6].
Check overlap: 4 >= 5 (false) → no overlap (second condition not needed). Return null.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Pairwise Comparison with Restart
ladders.desc: Repeatedly compare all interval pairs until no merges occur
ladders.explanation: The core logic implements the brute force strategy: keep comparing intervals until the list stabilizes with no overlaps. The intuition is that after merging two intervals, the new interval might overlap with others that were previously non-overlapping, so we must restart the comparison process. We use a while loop with a merged flag. Inside, nested for loops compare each interval i with all intervals j > i. If overlap is found, merge intervals i and j, replace interval i with the merged result, remove interval j, and set merged flag to true. Since removing j changes list indices and potentially creates new overlaps, we break the inner loop and restart the outer while loop. The algorithm: merged = true; while merged: merged = false; for i in 0 to n-2: for j in i+1 to n-1: if overlap(intervals[i], intervals[j]): intervals[i] = merge(intervals[i], intervals[j]); remove intervals[j]; n -= 1; merged = true; break. Time O(n²) as worst case needs n passes with O(n) comparisons each. Space O(1) in-place. This builds on L3's list modification. Common mistakes: not breaking after merge (causing index errors), not decrementing n, and infinite loops if flag not reset. Real code pattern: while True: merged=False; for i in range(len(intervals)-1): for j in range(i+1, len(intervals)): if overlap(intervals[i], intervals[j]): intervals[i]=merge(...); del intervals[j]; merged=True; break; if merged: break; if not merged: break.

examples.trace: Start: [[1,3],[2,6],[5,8]], n=3, merged=true. Pass1: Compare [1,3] and [2,6]: overlap, merge to [1,6], remove [2,6], list=[[1,6],[5,8]], n=2, merged=true, break. Restart. Pass2: Compare [1,6] and [5,8]: overlap, merge to [1,8], remove [5,8], list=[[1,8]], n=1, merged=true, break. Restart. Pass3: Only one interval, no compares, merged=false. Done.
Start: [[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]], n=3, merged=true. Pass1: Compare [1,2] and [3,4]: no overlap. Compare [1,2] and [5,6]: no overlap. Compare [3,4] and [5,6]: no overlap. merged=false. Done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modifying a List While Iterating
ladders.desc: Safely add, remove, or update elements in a list during iteration
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept handles the technical challenge of changing a list's structure while looping through it. The intuition: when we remove an element during iteration, subsequent elements shift positions, which can cause index errors or skipped elements if not managed carefully. In the brute force approach, after merging intervals i and j, we remove interval j and continue comparisons. The key is to adjust loop indices and list length accordingly. The algorithm: when removing an element at index j, decrement the list length variable n, and if using a for loop with index i, ensure i doesn't exceed new bounds. Often, we break the inner loop after a merge to restart the outer loop, simplifying index management. Alternatively, iterate backwards or use while loops with manual index control. Time O(n) for removal (shifting elements), space O(1). This connects to L2's restart logic. Common mistakes: not updating n after removal (leading to out-of-bounds access), not breaking loops (causing inconsistent state), and using for-each loops with removal (which throw errors). Real code pattern: n = len(intervals); i = 0; while i < n: j = i+1; while j < n: if condition: intervals[i] = new_value; del intervals[j]; n -= 1; break; else: j += 1; else: i += 1.

examples.input: Remove element at index 1 from list [10,20,30,40] while iterating
examples.trace: Start: list=[10,20,30,40], n=4, i=0. Inner loop j=1: condition true (e.g., overlap), remove index 1 (20), list becomes [10,30,40], n=3, break. Outer loop continues with i=0, new n=3 ensures j loops within bounds.

examples.input: Update element at index 0 and remove index 2 from [5,15,25,35]
examples.trace: Start: [5,15,25,35], n=4, i=0. Inner j=1: no condition. j=2: condition true, update index 0 to 10, remove index 2 (25), list becomes [10,15,35], n=3, break. Restart or continue based on algorithm.

testCases.input: Double each element and remove if >5 from [1,2,3]
Remove all elements after updating first to 0 from [10,20]
Insert at index and remove next from [x,y,z]
Update mid and remove end from [10,20,30]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Integer Pairs
ladders.desc: Determine the relationship between two intervals represented as [start,end]
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing two pairs of integers to check for overlap. The intuition: intervals are defined by start and end points, where start <= end. To compare, we look at their positions on a number line. The key comparison is checking if the intervals intersect: interval A [a,b] and interval B [c,d] overlap if b >= c AND a <= d. This ensures they share at least one point, including cases where they touch. The algorithm: function compare(interval1, interval2): return interval1[1] >= interval2[0] and interval1[0] <= interval2[1]. This is a boolean operation with O(1) time and space. This feeds into L3's condition for merging. Common mistakes: comparing only starts or ends, using strict inequalities (missing touching intervals), and not handling negative numbers correctly. Real code pattern: def overlaps(a,b): return a[1] >= b[0] and a[0] <= b[1].

examples.trace: Check: 3 >= 2 (true) and 1 <= 5 (true) → true (overlap).
Check: 6 >= 1 (true) but 4 <= 2 (false) → false (no overlap).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Intervals as Number Ranges
ladders.desc: Represent and interpret intervals as continuous ranges between two numbers
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding what an interval is. An interval represents all numbers between a start and end point, inclusive. For example, interval [1,3] includes numbers 1, 2, and 3. The intuition: intervals model continuous segments on a number line, useful for scheduling, ranges, etc. Key properties: start <= end (valid interval), and intervals can be compared based on their positions. The foundation involves: creating intervals as pairs [start,end], visualizing them on a number line, and identifying if two intervals are separate, touching, or overlapping. The algorithm: conceptually, draw intervals as lines; overlap means lines intersect or touch. Time and space are trivial for single intervals. This connects to L4's comparison. Common mistakes: thinking intervals are discrete points, confusing start/end order, and not including endpoints. Real code pattern: interval = [start, end] as list of two integers.

examples.trace: Start at 2, end at 5, all integers in between: 2,3,4,5.
[1,3] covers 1,2,3; [3,5] covers 3,4,5; they share point 3, so they touch.

approaches.name: Sort and Merge O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort intervals by start time, then iterate through them, merging overlapping intervals by comparing the current interval's start with the last merged interval's end. This is the standard optimal solution.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for output, or O(1) if sorted in-place

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge Intervals with Sort and Merge Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution: sort intervals by start time, then merge overlapping intervals by comparing starts with previous ends.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that by sorting intervals by their start times, we can process them in a single pass while maintaining a merged result list. When intervals are sorted, any interval that overlaps with the previous one will have a start time less than or equal to the previous interval's end time. This allows us to efficiently merge by extending the end time of the last merged interval when overlaps occur. The algorithm begins by sorting the intervals array based on the start value. If the input is empty, return an empty list. Initialize a result list with the first interval after sorting. Then iterate through the remaining intervals. For each interval, compare its start with the end of the last interval in the result list. If the start is less than or equal to the end, they overlap, so update the end of the last result interval to be the maximum of its current end and the current interval's end. If they don't overlap, append the current interval to the result list. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to sorting, plus O(n) for the linear scan, dominated by sorting. Space complexity is O(n) for the output list, or O(1) if sorted in-place and using the input list for output. This connects to L1 by using the merge logic as a subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling empty input, forgetting to sort, incorrectly comparing intervals (e.g., using less than instead of less than or equal for overlap), and not updating the end correctly when merging (should use max). Real code pattern: def merge(intervals): if not intervals: return []; intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0]); merged = [intervals[0]]; for current in intervals[1:]: last = merged[-1]; if current[0] <= last[1]: last[1] = max(last[1], current[1]); else: merged.append(current); return merged.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort intervals: already sorted as [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]. Step 2: Initialize merged = [[1,3]]. Step 3: Process [2,6]: last = [1,3], current start=2 <= last end=3 → overlap, update last end to max(3,6)=6, merged = [[1,6]]. Step 4: Process [8,10]: last = [1,6], current start=8 > last end=6 → no overlap, append, merged = [[1,6],[8,10]]. Step 5: Process [15,18]: last = [8,10], current start=15 > last end=10 → no overlap, append, merged = [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]. Output [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]].

Step 1: Sort intervals: already sorted as [[1,4],[4,5]]. Step 2: Initialize merged = [[1,4]]. Step 3: Process [4,5]: last = [1,4], current start=4 <= last end=4 → overlap (touching intervals merge), update last end to max(4,5)=5, merged = [[1,5]]. Output [[1,5]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Intervals Logic
ladders.desc: Determine if two intervals overlap and merge them into one if they do.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that two intervals overlap if the start of the second interval is less than or equal to the end of the first interval. When they overlap, the merged interval starts at the minimum of the two starts and ends at the maximum of the two ends. This sub-routine is the core merging logic used repeatedly in the full problem. The algorithm takes two intervals, a and b, assumed to be sorted by start time (a.start <= b.start). Check if b.start <= a.end. If true, they overlap, so return a new interval [a.start, max(a.end, b.end)]. If false, they do not overlap, so return the two intervals separately as [a, b]. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant-time comparisons and operations. Space complexity is O(1) for the output if returning new intervals, or O(1) if modifying in-place. This connects to L0 by being the operation applied during iteration after sorting. Common mistakes include not handling the case where intervals touch (b.start == a.end should merge), using strict less than for overlap check, and not considering that intervals might be unsorted (but in L0, sorting ensures a.start <= b.start). Real code pattern: def merge_two(a, b): if b[0] <= a[1]: return [[a[0], max(a[1], b[1])]]; else: return [a, b].

examples.trace: Step 1: Check overlap: b.start=2 <= a.end=3 → true. Step 2: Merge: start = min(1,2)=1, end = max(3,6)=6. Output [[1,6]].
Step 1: Check overlap: b.start=4 <= a.end=3 → false. Step 2: No merge, return both intervals. Output [[1,3],[4,6]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Merge After Sorting
ladders.desc: Process a sorted list of intervals, merging overlaps by comparing each interval with the last merged one.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that after sorting intervals by start time, any potential overlap can be detected by comparing the current interval's start with the end of the last interval in the result list. This allows us to build the merged list incrementally in one pass. The algorithm assumes the input list is already sorted by start time. Initialize an empty result list. If the input is not empty, add the first interval to result. Then iterate through the rest of the intervals. For each current interval, get the last interval in the result list. If the current start is less than or equal to the last end, they overlap, so update the last end to the maximum of last end and current end. Otherwise, append the current interval to the result list. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear scan. Space complexity is O(n) for the result list. This connects to L1 by using the two-interval merge logic in each step, and to L0 as the main loop after sorting. Common mistakes include not initializing result correctly for empty input, off-by-one errors in iteration, and not updating the end properly when merging (should use max). Real code pattern: def merge_sorted(intervals): if not intervals: return []; merged = [intervals[0]]; for i in range(1, len(intervals)): current = intervals[i]; last = merged[-1]; if current[0] <= last[1]: last[1] = max(last[1], current[1]); else: merged.append(current); return merged.

examples.trace: Step 1: merged = [[1,3]]. Step 2: Process [2,6]: last=[1,3], current start=2 <= last end=3 → overlap, update last end to max(3,6)=6, merged = [[1,6]]. Step 3: Process [8,10]: last=[1,6], current start=8 > last end=6 → no overlap, append, merged = [[1,6],[8,10]]. Step 4: Process [15,18]: last=[8,10], current start=15 > last end=10 → no overlap, append, merged = [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]. Output [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]].
Step 1: merged = [[1,4]]. Step 2: Process [4,5]: last=[1,4], current start=4 <= last end=4 → overlap, update last end to max(4,5)=5, merged = [[1,5]]. Output [[1,5]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting Intervals by Start Time
ladders.desc: Sort a list of intervals based on their start value to enable efficient merging.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that sorting intervals by their start time brings overlapping intervals close to each other, making it possible to merge them in a single pass. This is a prerequisite for the core logic because without sorting, we might need to compare every interval with every other interval, leading to O(n²) time. The algorithm involves defining a comparison key as the start value of each interval and using a standard sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort, mergesort). For a list of intervals, sort them in ascending order based on the first element. Time complexity is O(n log n) for comparison-based sorting. Space complexity is O(1) if sorted in-place, or O(n) if using extra space. This connects to L2 by providing the sorted input for the iterative merge. Common mistakes include sorting by the wrong key (e.g., end time), not handling custom objects correctly, and assuming input is already sorted. Real code pattern: intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0]) for a list of lists in Python.

examples.trace: Step 1: Extract start values: [8,15,1,2]. Step 2: Sort by start: order becomes indices 2,3,0,1. Step 3: Rearrange intervals: [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]].
Step 1: Extract start values: [1,0]. Step 2: Sort by start: order becomes indices 1,0. Step 3: Rearrange intervals: [[0,4],[1,4]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Interval Overlap
ladders.desc: Determine if two intervals overlap by comparing their start and end times.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that two intervals overlap if they share any common point in time. Mathematically, intervals [a, b] and [c, d] overlap if the start of one is less than or equal to the end of the other, assuming a <= b and c <= d. Specifically, they overlap if max(a, c) <= min(b, d). For simplicity, when intervals are sorted by start (a <= c), they overlap if c <= b. This operation is fundamental because merging depends on detecting overlap. The algorithm takes two intervals and returns True if they overlap, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) with constant-time comparisons. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 and above by being used in sorting and merging logic. Common mistakes include using strict inequality (c < b) which misses touching intervals, not assuming sorted order, and incorrect logic for unsorted intervals. Real code pattern: def overlaps(a, b): return max(a[0], b[0]) <= min(a[1], b[1]) for general case, or if a[0] <= b[0]: return b[0] <= a[1] else: return a[0] <= b[1] for sorted assumption.

examples.trace: Step 1: Assume sorted: a=[1,3], b=[2,6]. Step 2: Check b.start=2 <= a.end=3 → true. Output True.
Step 1: Assume sorted: a=[1,3], b=[4,6]. Step 2: Check b.start=4 <= a.end=3 → false. Output False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Intervals and Overlap
ladders.desc: Learn what intervals are and how to represent them, including the idea of overlap.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that an interval represents a continuous range of values, typically used for time periods or numerical ranges. In this context, an interval is defined by a start and an end value, inclusive, often represented as a pair [start, end] where start <= end. Overlap occurs when two intervals share at least one common value. For example, intervals [1,3] and [2,4] overlap because they both include values like 2 and 3. This foundational concept is crucial because the merge intervals problem is all about combining overlapping intervals into larger ones. To understand overlap, visualize intervals on a number line: if the start of one interval is less than or equal to the end of another, they overlap. This connects to L4 as the basis for comparison operations. Common mistakes include misunderstanding inclusive vs exclusive bounds (here, inclusive), not ensuring start <= end, and confusing overlap with containment. Real code pattern: Represent intervals as lists or tuples, e.g., interval = [start, end].

examples.trace: Step 1: Draw number line: [1,3] covers 1,2,3; [2,4] covers 2,3,4. Step 2: Check common values: 2 and 3 are in both. Step 3: Conclude overlap exists. Output Yes.
Step 1: Draw number line: [1,2] covers 1,2; [3,4] covers 3,4. Step 2: Check common values: none. Step 3: Conclude no overlap. Output No.

approaches.name: Graph Overlap Detection O(n²)
approaches.summary: Model intervals as nodes in a graph where edges represent overlaps, then find connected components to merge intervals within each component. This approach treats the problem as a graph connectivity challenge.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n²) for building the graph and O(n) for DFS/BFS
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n²) for adjacency list in worst case

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge Intervals via Graph Overlap Detection
ladders.desc: Complete solution using graph connectivity to merge overlapping intervals
ladders.explanation: This approach models intervals as nodes in an undirected graph where edges represent overlaps between intervals. The intuition is that overlapping intervals form connected components in this graph, and within each component, we can merge all intervals by taking the minimum start and maximum end. This transforms the interval merging problem into a graph connectivity problem. The algorithm proceeds in four main steps: 1) Build the graph by comparing every pair of intervals to detect overlaps. Two intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap if max(a,c) ≤ min(b,d). For each overlapping pair, add an edge between their corresponding nodes. 2) Perform DFS/BFS to find all connected components in the graph. 3) For each connected component, merge all intervals by taking min(start) and max(end). 4) Return the merged intervals. The time complexity is O(n²) for building the graph (comparing all pairs) plus O(n) for DFS/BFS traversal, totaling O(n²). Space complexity is O(n²) in worst case for the adjacency list when all intervals overlap with each other. This approach is conceptually elegant but less efficient than the optimal sorting approach, making it valuable for understanding graph applications. Common mistakes include: forgetting that intervals [1,2] and [2,3] overlap (they do, since max(1,2)=2 ≤ min(2,3)=2), incorrectly handling empty input, and not sorting merged intervals (though not required here, it's good practice). The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the graph building and component traversal to solve the complete problem.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph with 4 nodes (0:[1,3], 1:[2,6], 2:[8,10], 3:[15,18]). Compare pairs: (0,1): max(1,2)=2 ≤ min(3,6)=3 → overlap → add edge 0-1. (0,2): max(1,8)=8 > min(3,10)=3 → no overlap. (0,3): max(1,15)=15 > min(3,18)=3 → no overlap. (1,2): max(2,8)=8 > min(6,10)=6 → no overlap. (1,3): max(2,15)=15 > min(6,18)=6 → no overlap. (2,3): max(8,15)=15 > min(10,18)=10 → no overlap. Graph: {0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[], 3:[]}. Step 2: DFS to find components. Start at node 0: visit 0, then 1 → component {0,1}. Node 2 unvisited: component {2}. Node 3 unvisited: component {3}. Step 3: Merge components. Component {0,1}: intervals [1,3],[2,6] → min start=1, max end=6 → [1,6]. Component {2}: [8,10] → [8,10]. Component {3}: [15,18] → [15,18]. Step 4: Return [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]].

Step 1: Build graph with 2 nodes (0:[1,4], 1:[4,5]). Compare pairs: (0,1): max(1,4)=4 ≤ min(4,5)=4 → overlap → add edge 0-1. Graph: {0:[1], 1:[0]}. Step 2: DFS: start at node 0, visit 0 then 1 → component {0,1}. Step 3: Merge: intervals [1,4],[4,5] → min start=1, max end=5 → [1,5]. Step 4: Return [[1,5]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Connected Components in Interval Graph
ladders.desc: DFS/BFS traversal to identify connected components in the overlap graph
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine takes an adjacency list representing the overlap graph and returns a list of connected components. The intuition is that intervals that overlap (directly or transitively) belong to the same component, and we can discover all components through graph traversal. The algorithm: 1) Initialize visited array of size n (number of intervals) all false. 2) For each node i from 0 to n-1, if not visited, start a new component. 3) Perform DFS or BFS from node i, marking all reachable nodes as visited and adding them to the current component. 4) Append the component to the result list. 5) Return the list of components. Time complexity is O(n + e) where n is nodes and e is edges; since e can be O(n²) in worst case, this is O(n²). Space complexity is O(n) for visited array and recursion stack (or queue). This feeds into L0 by providing the components needed for merging. Common mistakes: not handling disconnected graphs properly (need to iterate through all nodes), infinite recursion in DFS (ensure visited check before recursion), and confusion between adjacency list representation (list of lists) and matrix. The real code pattern uses a simple DFS helper function.

examples.trace: Step 1: visited = [False, False, False]. Step 2: i=0 not visited → start component []. Step 3: DFS from 0: mark visited[0]=True, add 0 to component → component [0]. Neighbors of 0: [1] → DFS from 1: mark visited[1]=True, add 1 → component [0,1]. Neighbors of 1: [0,2] → 0 visited, DFS from 2: mark visited[2]=True, add 2 → component [0,1,2]. Neighbors of 2: [1] visited. Return. Step 4: Add component to result → [[0,1,2]]. Step 5: i=1 visited, i=2 visited. Return [[0,1,2]].

Step 1: visited = [False, False, False, False]. Step 2: i=0 not visited → start component []. Step 3: DFS from 0: mark visited[0]=True, add 0 → component [0]. Neighbors: [1] → DFS from 1: mark visited[1]=True, add 1 → component [0,1]. Neighbors of 1: [0] visited. Return. Step 4: Add [0,1] to result. Step 5: i=1 visited, i=2 not visited → start new component []. DFS from 2: mark visited[2]=True, add 2 → component [2]. Neighbors: [3] → DFS from 3: mark visited[3]=True, add 3 → component [2,3]. Neighbors of 3: [2] visited. Return. Add [2,3] to result. i=3 visited. Return [[0,1],[2,3]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Build Overlap Graph from Intervals
ladders.desc: Create adjacency list by comparing all interval pairs for overlaps
ladders.explanation: This core logic constructs an undirected graph where nodes represent intervals and edges exist between overlapping intervals. The intuition is that overlap detection is symmetric and transitive for merging purposes. The algorithm: 1) Initialize adjacency list as list of n empty lists. 2) For i from 0 to n-1: For j from i+1 to n-1 (to avoid duplicate edges and self-loops): Check if intervals[i] and intervals[j] overlap using condition max(start_i, start_j) ≤ min(end_i, end_j). 3) If overlap, add j to adj[i] and i to adj[j]. 4) Return adjacency list. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops comparing all pairs. Space complexity is O(n²) in worst case when all intervals overlap. This feeds into L1 by providing the graph for component detection. Common mistakes: using < instead of ≤ in overlap condition (intervals touching at a point like [1,2] and [2,3] should overlap), not handling empty intervals correctly (assume valid intervals with start ≤ end), and forgetting to add edges in both directions for undirected graph. The real code pattern uses simple list comprehensions and comparison.

examples.trace: n=2. Initialize adj = [[],[]]. i=0: interval [1,4]. j=1: [4,5] → max(1,4)=4 ≤ min(4,5)=4 → overlap → adj[0].append(1), adj[1].append(0) → adj=[[1],[0]]. Return [[1],[0]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Detect Overlap Between Two Intervals
ladders.desc: Determine if two intervals overlap using mathematical comparison
ladders.explanation: This building block checks whether two intervals [a,b] and [c,d] (with a≤b and c≤d) overlap. The intuition is that intervals overlap if they share at least one common point. Mathematically, this occurs when the maximum of the start points is less than or equal to the minimum of the end points: max(a,c) ≤ min(b,d). If true, intervals overlap; otherwise, they are disjoint. Step-by-step: 1) Compute max_start = max(a,c). 2) Compute min_end = min(b,d). 3) Return max_start ≤ min_end. Time complexity O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the overlap condition for graph building. Common mistakes: using < instead of ≤ (which would miss touching intervals like [1,2] and [2,3]), incorrectly assuming intervals are sorted, and not handling invalid intervals (but assume valid). The real code pattern is a simple boolean function.

examples.trace: Interval A: [1,3], Interval B: [2,6]. Step 1: max_start = max(1,2) = 2. Step 2: min_end = min(3,6) = 3. Step 3: Check 2 ≤ 3 → True. Return True.
Interval A: [8,10], Interval B: [15,18]. Step 1: max_start = max(8,15) = 15. Step 2: min_end = min(10,18) = 10. Step 3: Check 15 ≤ 10 → False. Return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Merge Two Overlapping Intervals
ladders.desc: Combine two overlapping intervals into one by taking min start and max end
ladders.explanation: This basic operation takes two intervals that are known to overlap and returns a single merged interval. The intuition is that when intervals overlap, their union spans from the earliest start to the latest end. The algorithm: 1) Given intervals [a,b] and [c,d] with max(a,c) ≤ min(b,d) (overlap condition). 2) Compute new_start = min(a,c). 3) Compute new_end = max(b,d). 4) Return [new_start, new_end]. Time and space complexity O(1). This feeds into L0 as part of merging components, but note in the graph approach we merge all intervals in a component at once. Common mistakes: assuming intervals are sorted (not necessary since we take min and max), not verifying overlap first (this operation is only valid for overlapping intervals), and incorrectly handling non-overlapping intervals (would produce incorrect merged interval). The real code pattern is straightforward arithmetic.

examples.trace: Interval A: [1,3], Interval B: [2,6]. Step 1: Verify overlap (from L3): max(1,2)=2 ≤ min(3,6)=3 → True. Step 2: new_start = min(1,2) = 1. Step 3: new_end = max(3,6) = 6. Step 4: Return [1,6].
Interval A: [1,4], Interval B: [4,5]. Step 1: Verify overlap: max(1,4)=4 ≤ min(4,5)=4 → True. Step 2: new_start = min(1,4) = 1. Step 3: new_end = max(4,5) = 5. Step 4: Return [1,5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Represent Intervals as Pairs of Numbers
ladders.desc: Understand intervals as [start, end] pairs with start ≤ end
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces intervals as fundamental mathematical objects represented by pairs of numbers [start, end] where start ≤ end. An interval includes all real numbers from start to end, inclusive. The intuition is that intervals model ranges or spans, such as time periods or numerical ranges. Key properties: 1) Start and end are numbers (integers or reals). 2) Start ≤ end; if equal, interval is a single point. 3) Interval length = end - start. 4) Intervals can be open/closed, but here we assume closed (including endpoints). This feeds into L4 by providing the basic data structure for merging. Common mistakes: confusing start and end order (always start ≤ end), treating intervals as unordered sets, and not understanding inclusive endpoints. The real code pattern uses lists or tuples to store pairs.

examples.trace: Given interval [1,3]. Step 1: Check start=1, end=3, and 1 ≤ 3 (valid). Step 2: The interval includes all integers from 1 to 3 inclusive: 1, 2, 3. Step 3: Length = 3 - 1 = 2. Step 4: Visualize on number line from 1 to 3.
Given interval [5,5]. Step 1: Check start=5, end=5, and 5 ≤ 5 (valid). Step 2: The interval includes only the number 5. Step 3: Length = 5 - 5 = 0. Step 4: Visualize as a point at 5 on number line.
